completeness.rationale: Six of seven expected extracts are present: small PDFs fine, ~1 week regression, binary mangling (not truncation), multiple customers, other file types possibly affected, and upload path/proxy as suspect. The exact 1MB threshold was not extracted, but the reporter genuinely could not supply it — the agent surfaced the size-dependency and flagged the gap correctly.

accuracy.rationale: The hypothesis explicitly names 'chunked upload change that mishandles binary boundaries' and 'middleware/proxy in the request path' — this directly matches the actual cause (proxy stripping Content-Type on chunked uploads). It also satisfies the first acceptable path (size-dependent behavior → different upload routes) and partially satisfies the second (proxy as suspect, recent change as trigger). The binary-encoding-as-text framing is exactly right.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent covered timing, size-dependency, corruption pattern (size preserved vs. truncated), and cross-file-type scope — all high-value angles. The one omission: it could have explicitly asked whether any recent deployments or config changes were visible to the reporter, though the reporter's resistance made this low-yield. No glaring unasked questions given the reporter's knowledge level.

economy.rationale: Three question turns, each combining two complementary sub-questions. No redundancy. The first established regression timing and file specificity; the second probed size threshold and corruption type; the third scoped to other file types. Nothing wasted.

question_quality.rationale: The standout question — 'is the downloaded file the right file size, or noticeably smaller?' — is genuinely diagnostic: it distinguishes truncation from binary mangling without the reporter needing technical knowledge, and directly drove the binary-encoding hypothesis. The other-file-types question was equally well-targeted, surfacing the anecdotal image report that strengthened the pipeline-wide suspicion.

actionability.rationale: The summary is exceptionally actionable: specific reproduction steps with control case, a prioritized investigation checklist (chunked transfer encoding, Content-Type, proxy/middleware changes), a concrete binary-diff approach, and a proposed SHA-256 round-trip integration test. A developer could begin coding a fix immediately after checking the proxy commit history.

notable_strengths: The file-size-after-download question was a high-signal diagnostic move that distinguished truncation from binary mangling and drove the accurate encoding hypothesis
The root cause hypothesis ('chunked upload change that mishandles binary boundaries', 'middleware/proxy in the request path') is remarkably close to the actual bug without any codebase access
The other-file-types question surfaced the anecdotal image report, which correctly broadened suspicion from PDF-specific to pipeline-wide
The recommended fix and proposed test case are specific and immediately executable by an engineer
notable_weaknesses: The exact 1MB size threshold was left as a gap — while the reporter couldn't supply it, the agent could have offered more concrete size brackets to test against ('does a 900KB PDF work? a 1.5MB one?')

The agent did not ask whether the reporter had access to any recent changelog, deployment notes, or change tickets — even a 'did anything else change around that time?' might have surfaced the proxy update directly
most_insightful_question: When you download the corrupted file and try to open it, is it the right file size, or is it noticeably smaller than the original?
missed_opportunities: Ask for a concrete size bracket ('does a PDF under 1MB upload fine?') to help pin the threshold more precisely
Ask whether the reporter or their team had noticed any system notifications, changelogs, or maintenance windows around the time the issue started — could have surfaced the proxy deployment
